pair.title: Show HN: VidStudio, a browser based video editor that doesn't upload your files

story.title: VidStudio - Free Online Video Resizer & Editor

comments.text: I tested this with a 4GB MP4 file on Chrome and Firefox. The browser-side processing actually works - took about 90 seconds to resize to 1080p and compress to ~300MB. No uploads, no server logs on my end (checked network tab). The WebAssembly implementation handles H.264 decoding reasonably well, though I hit some codec edge cases with an older iPhone

kids.text: The H.264 edge cases you hit are likely related to browser codec support variance. Safari on older iOS devices often chokes on certain profile/level combinations that Chrome handles fine. Did your iPhone file use Main or High profile? If it's High profile level 4.2+, that's probably why the

comments.text: The WebAssembly angle is the key differentiator here. Most browser-based video tools hit a wall around 500MB files before they start dropping frames or timing out. WebCodecs API gives you hardware-accelerated encoding, which is why this can actually handle the 4GB files that would ch

kids.text:
I tested this with a 2.3GB MP4 from a 2019 project and was genuinely shocked it worked without stuttering. Ran it on a 2015 MacBook Air, which I half-expected to choke on. The WebCodecs API really does make the difference, reminds me of when we first got GPU-accelerated compositing in browsers around 2010-2011. Back then everyone thought serious video work would always need desktop software.

One thing I noticed: the compression quality presets feel conservative compared to FFmpeg defaults, which is honestly the right call for a web tool. Lost maybe 15-20% file size vs aggressive settings, but the visual quality stayed solid. The frame extraction for thumbnails was

Tested this locally with a 2.8GB H.264 file on Chrome 131 / M1 Mac. Resizing to 1080p took about 90 seconds, which tracks with what you'd expect from WebCodecs doing the heavy lifting. The real win is that nothing left the browser, I could watch the file size drop in real time via the browser's memory profiler.

One caveat: Safari's WebCodecs support is still spotty depending on the codec. Tried the same file on Safari 18 and got a "format not supported" error. Firefox handled it fine though. So the "works on any device" claim needs an

WebCodecs is part of the story, but worth clarifying: WebCodecs itself doesn't do the encoding. It's a wrapper around the browser's native video codec (h264, vp8, etc.), which may or may not have hardware acceleration depending on the OS and GPU. The real win here is that you're not shipping a software encoder in WASM (which would be brutally slow).

The 500MB wall you mention is usually hit because of memory constraints in the JS heap, not codec speed. A 4GB file on a 32-bit browser is just not happening regardless of WebCodecs, so I'd want to see what the actual file size limits are in practice. Still impressive work if it handles gigabyte-range files, but the claim needs tightening.

Hmm, but doesn't this still rely on the browser having enough contiguous memory? I tested a similar tool last month with a 2GB file and it crashed on my iPad after about 90 seconds. The hardware acceleration helps, sure, but I'm skeptical the memory ceiling is really that high

comments.text: Interesting approach, but the "no upload" pitch glosses over a real problem: browser-side video processing maxes out around 2-4GB depending on device RAM, and fails silently on larger files. We ran into this hard limit building https://shotcut.org's web version last year.

kids.text: This reminds me of the whole Shotcut saga – I remember when they tried the browser route and hit the RAM wall hard. But there's an interesting flip side: most people resizing for social media are working with phone footage (100MB–1GB range), where browser processing actually shines. The real market might just be narrower than "everyone with a video file."

You're right about the market size. But there's a middle ground worth exploring: what if the tool pivots from "works for everything" to "works great for the specific 80% use case"? That's where https://ffmpeg.app or browser-based tools like Clipchamp carved out real usage.

The limitation isn't actually a bug if you market it honestly. "Fast resizing for social media up to 2GB" beats "uploads your video to our servers" every time for creators who just need a quick TikTok crop. Most phone videos *are* under 1GB anyway.

The silent failure is the

comments.text:
This is legitimately impressive. Browser-based video processing has always felt like a pipe dream due to codec complexity, but WebAssembly + WebCodecs actually makes it viable now.

The privacy angle is the real win here. Most "free" video tools are just datamining operations. Processing locally means no server logs, no metadata leaks, no mysterious file retention policies.

One question though: what's the memory ceiling? A 4GB file is manageable, but does it scale to the kind of sizes people actually work with on mobile? That's usually where the friction happens.

kids.text: WebCodecs is the unlock here, but the memory model still maxes out around 2-3GB on most browsers before you hit the garbage collection wall. Chrome's V8 is more forgiving than Safari's JSC. The real constraint isn't decoding-it's keeping both source and destination frames in memory

The memory ceiling is real, but the bigger issue is codec support variance across browsers. Safari still has spotty WebCodecs coverage, and Firefox's implementation lags Chrome. Have you tested on Safari with a 500MB+ file? That's where most "works great in my browser" tools fall apart.

I've tested this on Safari 17 with files up to 2GB and it holds up fine. The real Safari pain point is H.265 decoding, not memory. WebCodecs falls back to software decode on older Safari builds, which tanks performance but doesn't crash. Firefox's lagging codec
